# Doorman — complete context for LLMs and coding agents

This is a single self-contained document. An agent that has only this file can
correctly write, review, and debug code that uses Doorman. The authoritative
source of truth is the public header `doorman/include/doorman.h`; this file
restates it with the semantic contracts made explicit.

## 1. What Doorman is

Doorman (`libdoorman`) is a macOS-only C library that lets a program:

1. **Authenticate** a macOS user (verify a password) — either one-shot or via a
   PAM-style transaction with a conversation callback.
2. **Enumerate** users, resolve their group lists, and discover launchable
   login sessions (freedesktop `.desktop` entries + the built-in `aqua` entry).
3. **Open a session** as the authenticated user (privilege drop + login
   environment + exec), the way a display manager does.
4. **Provision** accounts: create/delete users and groups, set passwords,
   manage group membership, create home directories (Linux
   `useradd`/`passwd`/`groupadd` analogues; requires root).

It exists so Linux-shaped login software (display managers, greeters, account
tooling) can run on macOS without reimplementing OpenDirectory, dsLocal
shadow-hash parsing, or `dscl` shell-outs. Accounts it creates are ordinary
macOS accounts; the stock tools (`id`, `dscl`, `passwd`, Login Window) fully
interoperate.

Hard constraints:

- License: MIT (permissive; embeddable in proprietary software, attribution
  required).
- macOS only. It will not compile or run on Linux.
- C ABI. Consumable from C, Objective-C, C++ (header is C11- and C++17-clean),
  Swift (bridging header/module map), or any FFI (Rust, Go, Python ctypes...).
- Every API function returns `doorman_result_t`; `DOORMAN_SUCCESS == 0`.
- All mutation of the account database requires the caller to be root
  (euid 0); those functions return `DOORMAN_ERR_PERM` otherwise and have no
  side effects.

## 2. Install and link

Option A — prebuilt universal (arm64+x86_64) release from GitHub Releases:
`doorman-<version>-macos-universal.tar.gz` containing `lib/libdoorman.{a,dylib}`,
`include/doorman.h`, `bin/doorman` (+ Linux tool symlinks), `bin/macdm`.

Option B — Nix: `nix build .#doorman` (library), `.#doorman-cli`, `.#dist`.

Option C — make: `make && make test && sudo make install PREFIX=/usr/local`.

Compile and link (the exact required line):

    cc app.c -I<prefix>/include <prefix>/lib/libdoorman.a \
       -framework Foundation -framework OpenDirectory -framework Security \
       -lpam -lobjc

Include: `#include <doorman.h>` (or `"doorman.h"`). No other headers required
beyond standard C headers for your own code.

## 3. Result codes (returned by every function)

    typedef enum doorman_result {
        DOORMAN_SUCCESS = 0,       // operation completed successfully
        DOORMAN_ERR_AUTH,          // credentials were rejected
        DOORMAN_ERR_USER_UNKNOWN,  // no such user
        DOORMAN_ERR_ACCT_DISABLED, // account exists but cannot log in
        DOORMAN_ERR_PERM,          // caller lacks privileges (needs root)
        DOORMAN_ERR_CONV,          // conversation callback failed/aborted
        DOORMAN_ERR_ABORT,         // transaction is dead / wrong call order
        DOORMAN_ERR_NO_SESSION,    // no session open / session id not found
        DOORMAN_ERR_SYSTEM,        // underlying OS/directory error
        DOORMAN_ERR_INVALID_ARG,   // NULL/invalid argument
        DOORMAN_ERR_UNSUPPORTED,   // backend does not support the operation
    } doorman_result_t;

    const char *doorman_strerror(doorman_result_t result);
    // Static string, never NULL, never freed. Unknown values -> "unknown error".

## 4. Backends

    typedef enum doorman_backend {
        DOORMAN_BACKEND_AUTO = 0,        // OpenDirectory, falling back to the
                                         // dsLocal reader only on lookup/system
                                         // faults (never after a rejected password)
        DOORMAN_BACKEND_OPENDIRECTORY,   // ODRecord verifyPassword via
                                         // opendirectoryd; recommended default;
                                         // covers local + network/mobile accounts
        DOORMAN_BACKEND_DSLOCAL,         // parse /var/db/dslocal ShadowHashData,
                                         // verify SALTED-SHA512-PBKDF2 directly;
                                         // needs root to read the store; works
                                         // without opendirectoryd
        DOORMAN_BACKEND_PAM,             // drive /etc/pam.d/<service> via the
                                         // system OpenPAM; admin-configurable
    } doorman_backend_t;

Choosing: use `OPENDIRECTORY` (or `AUTO`) in production. Use `DSLOCAL` only in
early-boot/recovery contexts where opendirectoryd is unavailable (requires
root). Use `PAM` when the deployment wants `/etc/pam.d` policy control. The
one-shot helper does not support `PAM` (returns `DOORMAN_ERR_UNSUPPORTED`).

## 5. One-shot authentication (simplest entry point)

    doorman_result_t doorman_authenticate_password(const char *user,
                                                   const char *password,
                                                   doorman_backend_t backend);

- Verifies the pair, opens no session, needs no handle or callback.
- Returns SUCCESS / ERR_AUTH / ERR_USER_UNKNOWN / ERR_SYSTEM / ERR_INVALID_ARG,
  and ERR_UNSUPPORTED for the PAM backend.
- Works unprivileged with OPENDIRECTORY/AUTO. DSLOCAL needs root.

Recipe:

    if (doorman_authenticate_password("alice", pw, DOORMAN_BACKEND_AUTO)
            == DOORMAN_SUCCESS) {
        /* password is correct */
    }

## 6. Transactions (the PAM-shaped flow)

Opaque handle: `typedef struct doorman_handle doorman_handle_t;`

    doorman_result_t doorman_start(const char *service,      // NULL => "login"
                                   const char *user,         // NULL => conversation asks
                                   const doorman_conv_t *conv,
                                   doorman_backend_t backend,
                                   doorman_handle_t **out);
    void             doorman_end(doorman_handle_t *handle);  // NULL is a no-op

    doorman_result_t doorman_authenticate(doorman_handle_t *h);
    doorman_result_t doorman_acct_mgmt(doorman_handle_t *h);
    doorman_result_t doorman_setcred(doorman_handle_t *h, doorman_cred_flag_t flag);
    doorman_result_t doorman_open_session(doorman_handle_t *h,
                                          const doorman_session_t *session,
                                          pid_t *out_pid);
    doorman_result_t doorman_close_session(doorman_handle_t *h);

Required call order (enforced):

    doorman_start
      -> doorman_authenticate          (runs the conversation, verifies)
      -> doorman_acct_mgmt             (ERR_ABORT if called before a successful
                                        authenticate; checks disabled/expired)
      -> doorman_setcred(ESTABLISH)    (ERR_ABORT before successful authenticate;
                                        real pam_setcred for PAM backend,
                                        documented no-op returning SUCCESS for
                                        directory backends)
      -> doorman_open_session          (ERR_ABORT before successful authenticate;
                                        forks, drops privileges if root, execs)
      -> waitpid(child)
      -> doorman_close_session         (ERR_NO_SESSION if none open)
      -> doorman_end

Items (inspect/update a live handle):

    typedef enum doorman_item {
        DOORMAN_ITEM_SERVICE = 1, DOORMAN_ITEM_USER = 2,
        DOORMAN_ITEM_RHOST = 3, DOORMAN_ITEM_TTY = 4,
    } doorman_item_t;
    doorman_result_t doorman_set_item(doorman_handle_t*, doorman_item_t, const char *value);
    doorman_result_t doorman_get_item(doorman_handle_t*, doorman_item_t, const char **value);
    // get_item yields a BORROWED pointer (do not free); NULL if never set.
    // RHOST/TTY are forwarded to PAM as PAM_RHOST/PAM_TTY.

Credential flags:

    typedef enum doorman_cred_flag {
        DOORMAN_CRED_ESTABLISH = 1, DOORMAN_CRED_DELETE = 2,
        DOORMAN_CRED_REINITIALIZE = 3, DOORMAN_CRED_REFRESH = 4,
    } doorman_cred_flag_t;

## 7. The conversation callback (credential collection)

Doorman never takes a password parameter in the transaction flow; it calls back:

    typedef enum doorman_msg_style {
        DOORMAN_PROMPT_ECHO_OFF = 1,  // asking for a password (no echo)
        DOORMAN_PROMPT_ECHO_ON  = 2,  // asking for a username (echo)
        DOORMAN_ERROR_MSG       = 3,  // display error text, no input
        DOORMAN_TEXT_INFO       = 4,  // display info text, no input
    } doorman_msg_style_t;

    typedef struct doorman_message  { doorman_msg_style_t style; const char *msg; } doorman_message_t;
    typedef struct doorman_response { char *resp; int resp_retcode; } doorman_response_t;

    typedef int (*doorman_conv_fn)(int num_msg, const doorman_message_t **msg,
                                   doorman_response_t **resp, void *appdata);
    typedef struct doorman_conv { doorman_conv_fn conv; void *appdata; } doorman_conv_t;

Contract (critical for correctness):

- For every input-style message, allocate `resp[i]->resp` with `malloc`/`strdup`.
  Doorman TAKES OWNERSHIP: it scrubs (zero-wipes) and frees each response.
  Never pass a string literal, stack buffer, or a pointer you keep using.
- Return 0 on success; any non-zero return aborts with `DOORMAN_ERR_CONV`.
- If `doorman_start` got `user == NULL`, the conversation receives an extra
  `DOORMAN_PROMPT_ECHO_ON` message for the username.
- Values match Linux-PAM's constants, so an existing PAM conv function ports
  almost verbatim.

Canonical implementation:

    static int my_conv(int n, const doorman_message_t **msg,
                       doorman_response_t **resp, void *appdata) {
        (void)appdata;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            switch (msg[i]->style) {
            case DOORMAN_PROMPT_ECHO_OFF: resp[i]->resp = strdup(get_password()); break;
            case DOORMAN_PROMPT_ECHO_ON:  resp[i]->resp = strdup(get_username()); break;
            case DOORMAN_ERROR_MSG: fprintf(stderr, "%s\n", msg[i]->msg); break;
            case DOORMAN_TEXT_INFO: printf("%s\n", msg[i]->msg); break;
            }
        }
        return 0;
    }

## 8. Users and groups (read-only; no root needed)

    typedef struct doorman_user {
        char *name, *full_name, *home, *shell;  // heap strings, may be NULL
        uid_t uid; gid_t gid;
        bool hidden;                            // system/service account
    } doorman_user_t;

    doorman_result_t doorman_enumerate_users(bool interactive_only,
                                             doorman_user_t **out, size_t *count);
    void             doorman_free_users(doorman_user_t *users, size_t count);
    doorman_result_t doorman_lookup_user(const char *name, doorman_user_t *out);
    void             doorman_free_user_fields(doorman_user_t *user);
    doorman_result_t doorman_get_groups(const char *user, gid_t **gids, size_t *count);

- `interactive_only == true` filters out service accounts (uid < 500,
  `_`-prefixed names, non-login shells) — i.e. what a login screen shows.
- `doorman_lookup_user` fills a caller-provided struct; release its members
  with `doorman_free_user_fields` (not `doorman_free_users`).
- `doorman_get_groups` returns a heap `gid_t` array (primary gid first);
  release with plain `free()`.

## 9. Sessions (discovery + launch)

    typedef struct doorman_session {
        char *id;      // desktop-file basename, or "aqua"
        char *name;    // Name= from the desktop entry
        char *comment; // Comment=, may be NULL
        char *exec;    // Exec= command line
        char *type;    // "wayland" | "x11" | "aqua"
    } doorman_session_t;

    doorman_result_t doorman_enumerate_sessions(doorman_session_t **out, size_t *count);
    void             doorman_free_sessions(doorman_session_t *sessions, size_t count);

- Reads `wayland-sessions/` and `xsessions/` under `$XDG_DATA_DIRS` (defaults
  to `/usr/local/share:/usr/share`) and always appends the built-in `aqua`
  entry, so the result is never empty.

`doorman_open_session(h, session, &pid)` (transaction API, see §6):

- Requires a successful `doorman_authenticate` first (`ERR_ABORT` otherwise);
  `session->exec` and `session->type` must be non-NULL (`ERR_INVALID_ARG`).
- If the caller is root: forks, drops privileges (`setgid`/`initgroups`/
  `setuid`), calls `setsid`, sets a minimal login environment (USER, LOGNAME,
  HOME, SHELL, PATH, XDG_RUNTIME_DIR, XDG_SESSION_TYPE from `session->type`,
  XDG_SESSION_DESKTOP from `session->id`, and WAYLAND_DISPLAY for wayland
  sessions), and execs the user's login shell as `<shell> -l -c <exec>`.
- If the caller is NOT root: it can only launch a session for the current
  user (useful for development); targeting a different uid returns
  `DOORMAN_ERR_PERM`.
- Returns immediately with the child pid in `*out_pid` (if non-NULL). The
  caller waits on the pid, then calls `doorman_close_session(h)`.
- A caller may construct its own `doorman_session_t` on the stack (set `id`,
  `name`, `exec`, `type`) instead of using an enumerated one.

## 10. Provisioning (requires root; Linux useradd/passwd analogues)

    typedef struct doorman_user_spec {
        const char *name;       // REQUIRED login name
        const char *full_name;  // default: name
        const char *password;   // NULL => no password set
        const char *home;       // default: /Users/<name>
        const char *shell;      // default: /bin/zsh
        uid_t uid;              // 0 => auto-assign next free uid >= 501
        gid_t gid;              // 0 => 20 (staff)
        bool admin;             // also add to the admin group
        bool hidden;            // hidden/service account
        bool create_home;       // create home from the macOS user template
    } doorman_user_spec_t;

    doorman_result_t doorman_create_user(const doorman_user_spec_t *spec);
    doorman_result_t doorman_delete_user(const char *name, bool remove_home);
    doorman_result_t doorman_set_password(const char *name, const char *new_password);
    doorman_result_t doorman_create_home(const char *name);              // idempotent
    doorman_result_t doorman_create_group(const char *name, gid_t gid,   // gid 0 => auto
                                          const char *full_name);        // may be NULL
    doorman_result_t doorman_delete_group(const char *name);
    doorman_result_t doorman_add_user_to_group(const char *user, const char *group);
    doorman_result_t doorman_remove_user_from_group(const char *user, const char *group);

Rules:

- All of these require euid 0; otherwise `DOORMAN_ERR_PERM`, no side effects.
- Names are strictly validated: only ASCII letters, digits, `.`, `_`, `-`;
  must not start with `-` or `.`; max 244 chars. Invalid names return
  `DOORMAN_ERR_INVALID_ARG` before anything is attempted.
- Passwords are set through the OpenDirectory API, never passed on a command
  line, so they cannot leak via `ps`.
- Accounts are ordinary macOS accounts (written via the native store); `id`,
  `dscl`, `passwd`, System Settings, and Login Window all see them.

Recipe (must run as root):

    doorman_user_spec_t spec = {
        .name = "builder", .full_name = "CI Builder",
        .password = "S3cret!", .create_home = true,
    };
    doorman_create_user(&spec);
    doorman_create_group("builders", 0, "Builders");
    doorman_add_user_to_group("builder", "builders");
    doorman_set_password("builder", "N3wSecret!");
    doorman_remove_user_from_group("builder", "builders");
    doorman_delete_group("builders");
    doorman_delete_user("builder", /*remove_home=*/true);

## 11. Complete login-flow recipe (display-manager shape)

    #include <doorman.h>
    #include <sys/wait.h>

    doorman_conv_t conv = { my_conv, NULL };
    doorman_handle_t *h = NULL;
    doorman_result_t r = doorman_start("login", username, &conv,
                                       DOORMAN_BACKEND_OPENDIRECTORY, &h);
    if (r != DOORMAN_SUCCESS) goto out;

    r = doorman_authenticate(h);                  // prompts via conv
    if (r != DOORMAN_SUCCESS) goto out;           // ERR_AUTH => wrong password
    r = doorman_acct_mgmt(h);                     // ERR_ACCT_DISABLED => locked
    if (r != DOORMAN_SUCCESS) goto out;
    doorman_setcred(h, DOORMAN_CRED_ESTABLISH);

    doorman_session_t *sessions = NULL; size_t n = 0;
    doorman_enumerate_sessions(&sessions, &n);
    pid_t pid = 0;
    r = doorman_open_session(h, &sessions[choice], &pid);
    if (r == DOORMAN_SUCCESS) {
        int status; waitpid(pid, &status, 0);
        doorman_close_session(h);
    }
    doorman_free_sessions(sessions, n);
    out:
    if (r != DOORMAN_SUCCESS) fprintf(stderr, "%s\n", doorman_strerror(r));
    doorman_end(h);

## 12. Memory-ownership rules (exact)

| You got it from | You release it with |
|---|---|
| `doorman_start` | `doorman_end` |
| `doorman_enumerate_users` | `doorman_free_users(arr, count)` |
| `doorman_lookup_user` (fills your struct) | `doorman_free_user_fields(&u)` |
| `doorman_enumerate_sessions` | `doorman_free_sessions(arr, count)` |
| `doorman_get_groups` | `free(gids)` |
| `doorman_get_item` | nothing — borrowed pointer, do not free |
| `doorman_strerror` | nothing — static string |
| conversation `resp[i]->resp` you allocated | nothing — Doorman scrubs + frees it |

## 13. Threading and process rules

- A `doorman_handle_t` must be driven by one thread at a time. Independent
  handles on different threads are fine.
- Read-only calls (`enumerate_*`, `lookup_user`, `get_groups`, `strerror`) are
  reentrant.
- `doorman_open_session` forks; the child performs only async-signal-safe work
  before `exec` (the login environment is prebuilt in the parent).

## 14. CLI equivalents (binary: `doorman`)

    doorman authenticate <user>                 verify a password (reads stdin)
    doorman login <user> [--exec CMD] [--session ID]
    doorman useradd [-m -u UID -g GID -s SHELL -c GECOS -d HOME -p PW -G GRP --admin --hidden] <name>
    doorman userdel [-r] <name>
    doorman passwd [--stdin] <user>
    doorman groupadd [-g gid] <name>
    doorman groupdel <name>
    doorman usermod -aG <group> <user>
    doorman gpasswd -a|-d <user> <group>
    doorman users | sessions | groups <user>

The binary also answers directly to the names `useradd`, `userdel`, `passwd`,
`groupadd`, `groupdel`, `usermod`, `gpasswd` (argv[0] symlinks), so Linux
account scripts run unchanged.

## 15. Common mistakes to avoid

1. Passing a non-heap string as a conversation response (Doorman frees it).
2. Freeing the pointer from `doorman_get_item` or `doorman_strerror`.
3. Calling `acct_mgmt`/`setcred`/`open_session` before a successful
   `doorman_authenticate` — they return `DOORMAN_ERR_ABORT` by design.
4. Using `doorman_authenticate_password` with `DOORMAN_BACKEND_PAM` — returns
   `DOORMAN_ERR_UNSUPPORTED`; PAM requires the transaction + conversation flow.
5. Expecting provisioning or `DSLOCAL` verification to work without root.
6. Expecting `doorman_open_session` to switch users when the process is not
   root — privilege dropping requires starting privileged.
7. Releasing a `doorman_lookup_user` result with `doorman_free_users` (wrong:
   that is for enumeration arrays); use `doorman_free_user_fields`.
8. Forgetting the link frameworks: Foundation, OpenDirectory, Security, plus
   `-lpam -lobjc`.
